Screener Q: Now, I'd like to switch gears a bit.
When you're planning a multi-city international trip, how do you typically approach the very first decisions about destinations?
[Speaker:A] I think for me, maybe I do some research on like TikTok, or I just ask my friends like where they've been and where they recommend to go.
But usually I have a good idea of where I want to go next and what I want to explore.
So it's, that's kind of an easy part.
Screener Q: And once you have an idea of where you want to go, what's the very next step you take in planning that multi-city international trip?
A: Yeah, I mean, I already showed you, but I would look for flights so I can kind of just establish the dates correctly.
Screener Q: And how do you prioritize the order of cities or destinations when you're planning a multi-city international trip?
A: I mean, you just got to be clever, right?
Like, you can't book a city that's 2 hours away.
I mean, like, 10 hours away if you can go to the city that's just next door.
So you just got to be a bit clever about the distance.
Can you give me an example of a time you were clever about the distance when planning a multi-city international trip?
I can't give you an exact example, but I'm sure I would have just looked at a map and then been like, oh, these two cities are right next to each other, so I can go visit this one straight after.
Q: So if you can't recall a specific example, how do you typically approach that process of looking at a map and deciding on the order of cities?
A: Literally just like that.
Just look at a map, say I want to go to London, I'll be like, oh, this city is right next to London, I might just pop in.
That's kind of a decision I've made, and then I start booking.
S: Okay, so you identify a main destination, then look for nearby cities.
Q: When does budget typically come into play during this initial stage of planning a multi-city international trip?

A: Budget's like the first thing I've already thought of, so my budget is already in mind before this whole process, but it can also change because I feel like everything's just constantly becoming more expensive, so my budget always increases slightly more than I was expecting.
So your budget is a predetermined factor, but it's also flexible.
Q: How do you account for potential budget increases as you're making those early decisions about destinations and travel order?
[Speaker:A] I mean, I have savings, so I account for them, but I don't want to overspend either.
[Speaker:S] And how does the timing of the trip, like the time of year or specific dates, influence your initial decisions about destinations and the order of travel?
[Speaker:A] I think it massively impacts it because I don't want to travel to a place where it's just not good to go there when it's cold.
So that's a massive factor.

Can you give me an example of a time when the weather or seasonality significantly influenced your decision of where not to go or the order in which you visited cities on a multi-city international trip?
It hasn't affected any order of cities, but I would say I've decided to go to Europe during summer, not winter.
Screener Q: I'd like to understand how you decide which websites or platforms you use for researching and booking travel.
Can you tell me what typically leads you to choose a specific website or app when you start planning a trip?

Not really.
It just, if anything, just helped me book quickly.

Is there anything else about them that influences your decision to choose them for booking?
No.
No.
